content.text: Internal system integration connects an agent to infrastructure your organisation controls: a CRM, an internal REST API, an HR system, a data warehouse, or a message queue. Unlike third-party APIs, you own the server and the network path — which means you control the credentials, the schema, and the access model. That control is an advantage, but it also makes the access decisions yours to get right.

attrs.alt: Internal system integration trust zones: agent layer, internal systems, legacy/sensitive tier
attrs.title: Internal System Trust Zones

content.text: The agent should operate with the minimum privilege a human assistant in the same role would have — not the permissions of the service account's owner, and certainly not admin access.

An agent calling an internal API uses a dedicated service account, not a developer's personal token. The service account has only the permissions needed for the operations the agent can perform. Its credentials live in environment variables or a secrets manager and are injected in the handler — never in the tool schema the model sees.

import os
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from urllib3.util.retry import Retry

_INTERNAL_BASE = os.environ["INTERNAL_API_URL"]       # e.g. https://api.internal.corp
_AGENT_TOKEN   = os.environ["INTERNAL_AGENT_TOKEN"]   # service-account JWT

def _make_session() -> requests.Session:
    session = requests.Session()
    session.headers.update({
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {_AGENT_TOKEN}",
        "X-Agent-Id": "support-agent-v1",   # auditing: which agent made this call
    })
    adapter = HTTPAdapter(
        max_retries=Retry(
            total=3,
            backoff_factor=0.4,
            status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504],
            allowed_methods=["GET"],  # only retry read operations automatically
        )
    )
    session.mount("https://", adapter)
    session.mount("http://", adapter)
    return session

_SESSION = _make_session()
header identifies the agent in server-side access logs. When investigating why a record changed, you can filter logs by this header to see exactly which agent did it and when.
A read tool for an internal CRM follows the same pruning discipline as a third-party wrapper: fetch what the system returns, return only what the model needs.
even if the CRM returns them. The model does not need PII to answer "is this account active?" — limiting what reaches the context window also limits exposure if the conversation is logged.

Write operations against internal systems require tighter guard rails than reads. Validate before mutating, use an allowlist for writable fields, and require an explicit reason string so the audit log captures why the change was made.
field is written to the internal audit log. When auditors ask why an account was suspended, the agent's stated reason appears in the record alongside the timestamp and the service account ID.

Data warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) are read-only for most agent use cases. Expose them through named, parameterised queries rather than letting the model construct SQL — this prevents injection and keeps the tool schema self-documenting.
prevent injection: the driver sends the query template and the parameters separately; BigQuery substitutes them server-side. The model never builds a SQL string.

Some internal integrations do not call a synchronous API but instead publish an event to a message queue (Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ). The queue decouples the agent from the downstream system — the agent does not wait for the downstream process to finish.
field in the event body identifies the agent in downstream logs, even after the message has left the agent process. The
(a UUID generated per call) is used as the SQS deduplication ID, which prevents the FIFO queue from processing duplicate events if the tool is accidentally called twice.

When an internal service is repeatedly unavailable, continuing to call it slows every tool call in the agent loop and generates noise in the service's logs. A simple circuit breaker stops issuing requests after consecutive failures and resets after a cooldown.
rather than sending a request that will fail anyway.

Every write operation against an internal system should be logged to an audit trail that persists independently of application logs. The log entry should capture what changed, why, which agent made the change, and when.

import logging
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

_AUDIT = logging.getLogger("agent.audit")
# Configure _AUDIT to write to an append-only sink (e.g. CloudWatch, Splunk, a WORM bucket).

def _audit_write(
    action: str,
    resource_type: str,
    resource_id: str,
    before: dict | None,
    after: dict | None,
    reason: str,
) -> None:
    _AUDIT.info(json.dumps({
        "ts":            datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "agent":         "support-agent-v1",
        "action":        action,          # e.g. "update_account_status"
        "resource_type": resource_type,   # e.g. "crm_customer"
        "resource_id":   resource_id,
        "before":        before,
        "after":         after,
        "reason":        reason,
    }))
inside write handlers after the system confirms the change. Capturing
state makes the log entry self-contained: auditors can reconstruct the change without fetching the current record.

Test internal integrations with mocked sessions and fixture objects — not against a shared staging environment. Staging environments drift, change unexpectedly, and introduce cross-test contamination.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
from mymodule import get_customer_record, update_account_status

def _mock_resp(status: int, body: dict) -> MagicMock:
    r = MagicMock()
    r.status_code = status
    r.ok = status < 400
    r.json.return_value = body
    return r

_FULL_CUSTOMER = {
    "id": "C001", "name": "Acme Corp", "tier": "enterprise",
    "status": "active", "open_tickets": 3,
    "account_manager_name": "Bob", "created_at": "2023-01-15",
    "ssn": "000-00-0000",       # should be pruned
    "date_of_birth": "1990-01-01",  # should be pruned
}

def test_get_customer_prunes_pii():
    with patch("mymodule._SESSION") as m:
        m.get.return_value = _mock_resp(200, _FULL_CUSTOMER)
        result = get_customer_record("C001")
    assert "ssn" not in result
    assert "date_of_birth" not in result
    assert result["account_name"] == "Acme Corp"

def test_update_status_conflict():
    with patch("mymodule._SESSION") as m:
        m.patch.return_value = _mock_resp(409, {"detail": "already active"})
        result = update_account_status("C001", "active", "Reinstating account")
    assert result["error"] == "CONFLICT"

def test_update_status_invalid_reason():
    result = update_account_status("C001", "suspended", "short")
    assert result["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert "10 characters" in result["message"]
) is particularly important: it verifies that fields you intentionally excluded from the return value are actually absent, not silently forwarded to the model.
Use a dedicated service account with minimum privilege; inject credentials from environment variables, not the tool schema.
Prune PII and irrelevant fields before returning data to the model — what leaves the handler is what enters the model's context window.
parameter on write tools and pass it to the internal audit log so every change is traceable.
Use parameterised queries for data warehouse tools — never let the model construct SQL strings.
A circuit breaker prevents cascading failures when an internal service is down; surface SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE immediately once the breaker is open.
Test with mocked sessions and fixture data; the PII-pruning test is as important as the happy-path test.
